references.title: Managing extreme AI risks amid rapid progress
references.abstract: Policy leaders and researchers outline a governance agenda to reduce extreme risks from rapidly advancing AI, emphasizing capability evaluations, monitoring, safety research, and coordinated international oversight.

references.title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
references.abstract: Identifies five practical classes of safety problems for advanced ML systems—negative side effects, reward hacking, scalable oversight, safe exploration, and robustness to distributional shift—and proposes experiments and research directions.

references.title: The Malicious Use of Artificial Intelligence: Forecasting, Prevention, and Mitigation
references.abstract: Surveys ways AI may be misused across digital, physical, and political domains and recommends forecasting, prevention, and mitigation strategies to reduce societal-scale harms.

references.title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
references.abstract: Analyzes environmental, social, and epistemic risks of large LMs and calls for documentation, dataset curation, stakeholder-aligned development, and governance to manage harms.

references.title: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models
references.abstract: Provides a comprehensive, interdisciplinary analysis of foundation models’ capabilities, risks, and societal impacts, arguing for sociotechnical research and governance to guide development and deployment.

references.title: Ethical and social risks of harm from Language Models
references.abstract: Synthesizes 21 ethical and social risk areas for large language models—including discrimination, information hazards, misinformation, malicious use, HCI risks, and environmental impacts—and outlines mitigation and governance responsibilities.

references.title: Model evaluation for extreme risks
references.abstract: Argues for standardized dangerous capability and alignment evaluations to assess and govern frontier AI models, informing deployment, policy, and security decisions.

references.title: Evaluating Frontier Models for Dangerous Capabilities
references.abstract: Introduces and pilots a suite of evaluations for persuasion, cybersecurity, self-proliferation, and self-reasoning on Gemini models, offering an empirical basis for governance of dangerous capabilities.

references.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
references.abstract: Demonstrates that reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) aligns LMs with user intent, improving helpfulness and reducing harmfulness—a key technique for managing advanced systems.

references.title: The global landscape of AI ethics guidelines
references.abstract: Reviews 84 AI ethics guidelines, finding convergence on principles like transparency and responsibility but divergence in interpretation and implementation, underscoring the need for actionable governance.

references.title: Managing extreme AI risks amid rapid progress
references.abstract: Policy Forum outlining technical and governance measures to address potential large-scale and catastrophic risks from increasingly capable AI systems, arguing for proactive evaluation, standards, and oversight. Provides a consensus roadmap by leading researchers on coordinating safety research with adaptive regulation.

references.title: Don’t pause giant AI for the wrong reasons
references.abstract: Editorial arguing that evidence for a blanket moratorium on training more powerful AI systems was insufficient, urging risk-proportionate governance and empirical evaluation instead of time-bound pauses. Critiques core premises of the 2023 open letter calling for a six-month pause.

references.title: The Vulnerable World Hypothesis
references.abstract: Proposes a framework for assessing how future technologies could create systemic civilizational vulnerabilities and argues that extraordinary governance measures may sometimes be needed to prevent catastrophic misuse. Offers conceptual grounding often cited in debates about slowing or restricting risky technological development.

references.title: Coordinated pausing: An evaluation-based coordination scheme for frontier AI developers
references.abstract: Proposes concrete mechanisms for developers to pause certain AI R&D activities when dangerous capabilities are detected by evaluations, and to resume only after safety thresholds are met. Discusses voluntary, contractual, auditor-led, and legally mandated variants along with antitrust considerations.

references.title: Frontier AI Regulation: Managing Emerging Risks to Public Safety
references.abstract: Outlines a regulatory toolkit for highly capable foundation models, including safety evaluations, incident reporting, and compute-based controls, to mitigate public-safety risks. Provides governance options relevant to proposals for temporary pauses or conditional restrictions.

references.title: An Overview of Catastrophic AI Risks
references.abstract: Surveys pathways to catastrophic harm from advanced AI, including malicious use, competitive pressures, organizational failures, and loss of control, and proposes mitigation strategies. Synthesizes risk arguments that often motivate calls for slowing or pausing AI development.

references.title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
references.abstract: Analyzes environmental, social, and epistemic risks from scaling large language models and recommends documentation, data curation, and alternative research incentives. Often cited as motivating caution and governance interventions around rapid LLM scaling.

references.title: The Malicious Use of Artificial Intelligence: Forecasting, Prevention, and Mitigation
references.abstract: Forecasts near-term security threats from AI across digital, physical, and political domains and recommends preemptive defenses and norms. Provides an early, widely cited foundation for anticipating misuse risks during periods of rapid capability growth.

references.title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
references.abstract: Identifies practical safety problems in modern ML systems—reward hacking, negative side effects, scalable oversight, safe exploration, and robustness to distributional shift—and suggests research directions. A seminal technical agenda underpinning arguments for dedicating time to build safeguards.

references.title: Computing Power and the Governance of Artificial Intelligence
references.abstract: Argues that compute is a tractable lever for AI governance because it is measurable and controllable, outlining policy tools such as monitoring, allocation, and enforcement via compute. Provides alternatives and complements to blanket pauses through targeted restrictions and oversight.

references.title: GPT-4 Technical Report
references.abstract: OpenAI describes the development and evaluation of GPT-4, a large-scale multimodal model that attains strong, often human-level, performance on diverse professional and academic benchmarks, highlighting rapid capability gains from scaling.

references.title: Sparks of Artificial General Intelligence: Early experiments with GPT-4
references.abstract: Microsoft researchers report broad, near-human performance of early GPT-4 across mathematics, coding, law, medicine, and more, arguing that its breadth and depth of capabilities mark a qualitative leap over prior systems.

references.title: Compute Trends Across Three Eras of Machine Learning
references.abstract: Analyzing training compute for milestone ML models since 1950, the authors identify three eras with accelerating growth—showing post‑2010 doubling times far faster than Moore’s law and a large‑scale era with 10–100× jumps—quantifying the unprecedented pace of frontier training runs.
references.venue: International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN)

references.title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
references.abstract: DeepMind shows that many large LMs were undertrained on tokens and introduces compute‑optimal scaling (Chinchilla), demonstrating substantially improved accuracy at the same training compute—evidencing rapid capability gains from optimization and scaling.

references.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
references.abstract: Kaplan et al. establish power‑law relationships between language model performance and scale in parameters, data, and compute, providing a framework that predicted rapid improvements as resources increased.

references.title: Measuring the Algorithmic Efficiency of Neural Networks
references.abstract: Hernandez and Brown quantify algorithmic progress by showing large reductions in compute needed to reach past results (e.g., 44× for ImageNet 2012–2019), implying faster‑than‑hardware capability gains.

references.title: Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models
references.abstract: Wei et al. document that certain capabilities appear abruptly at larger scales and are hard to predict from smaller models, underscoring the surprising, rapid qualitative shifts seen as LLMs scale.
references.venue: Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR); arXiv preprint

references.title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM)
references.abstract: HELM proposes a broad, multi‑metric framework and reports large‑scale comparative results across scenarios, showing rapid, uneven capability growth and surfacing risks as models advance quickly.

references.title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
references.abstract: Bender et al. critique the rapid scaling of language models, detailing environmental, social, and epistemic risks that accompany the fast pace of development and deployment.
references.venue: ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT)

references.title: Large language models encode clinical knowledge
references.abstract: Using the MultiMedQA benchmark and human evaluation, Singhal et al. show that scaled LLMs (e.g., Flan‑PaLM/Med‑PaLM) achieve strong results on medical QA, illustrating rapid, cross‑domain capability advances while highlighting safety gaps.

references.title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
references.abstract: Identifies five practical research problems—avoiding negative side effects, reward hacking, scalable oversight, safe exploration, and robustness to distributional shift—to reduce accident risks in real-world ML systems and guide collaboration between researchers and practitioners.

references.title: The Malicious Use of Artificial Intelligence: Forecasting, Prevention, and Mitigation
references.abstract: Surveys ways AI could be misused across digital, physical, and political domains and offers recommendations for policymakers, industry, and researchers to forecast, prevent, and mitigate emerging threats.

references.title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
references.abstract: Warns that ever-larger language models risk environmental costs, entrenched biases, and misuse, and argues for documentation, dataset curation, and governance to ensure responsible development.
references.venue: ACM FAccT (Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency)

references.title: Managing extreme AI risks amid rapid progress
references.abstract: A consortium of leading AI researchers outlines technical and governance measures to address extreme risks from increasingly autonomous, general-purpose AI systems, urging proactive expert-informed oversight.

references.title: The Off-Switch Game
references.abstract: Analyzes when a rational AI agent would allow itself to be shut down and shows that appropriate uncertainty over objectives can incentivize preserving human oversight mechanisms like an off‑switch.

references.title: Optimal Policies Tend to Seek Power
references.abstract: Provides a formal analysis showing that, in many environments, optimal policies tend to preserve options and avoid shutdown, implying power‑seeking incentives that pose safety concerns for capable agents.
references.venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS)

references.title: Society-in-the-loop: programming the algorithmic social contract
references.abstract: Proposes a governance framework that combines human‑in‑the‑loop control with social contract mechanisms so stakeholders and experts can negotiate values and monitor compliance of AI systems.

references.title: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models
references.abstract: Comprehensive report on capabilities, risks, and societal impacts of large foundation models, emphasizing the need for interdisciplinary research, evaluation, and governance to manage harms.

references.title: Existential Risk Prevention as Global Priority
references.abstract: Defines existential risks and argues that reducing anthropogenic, technology‑driven extinction risks has immense expected value, motivating early expert analysis and coordinated policy responses.

references.title: The Vulnerable World Hypothesis
references.abstract: Posits that technological progress may expose civilization‑destabilizing ‘black ball’ inventions and argues for strengthened global governance and preventive oversight to manage such risks.

references.title: Prevalence of Online Sexual Offenses Against Children in the US
references.abstract: A nationally representative survey of 2,639 U.S. young adults found substantial childhood exposure to online sexual abuse, including grooming, image-based sexual abuse, and sextortion, underscoring the scope of technology-facilitated harms to minors.

references.title: The Prevalence of Unwanted Online Sexual Exposure and Solicitation Among Youth: A Meta-Analysis
references.abstract: Meta-analysis of 31 samples (n=37,649) and 9 samples (n=18,272) estimated that about 1 in 5 youth experience unwanted online sexual exposure and about 1 in 9 encounter online sexual solicitation, highlighting persistent digital risks.

references.title: Sextortion of Minors: Characteristics and Dynamics
references.abstract: Analysis of 1,385 sextortion victims compared minors and young adults, documenting tactics, threats, and outcomes; minors faced serious harms and coercion, emphasizing the need for targeted prevention and response.

references.title: Sextortion Among Adolescents: Results From a National Survey of U.S. Youth
references.abstract: In a nationally representative survey of 5,568 U.S. students aged 12–17, 5% reported victimization and 3% perpetration of sextortion, with notable gender patterns—evidence that sextortion is a significant youth safety issue.

references.title: Assessing image‐based sexual abuse: Measurement, prevalence, and temporal stability of sextortion and nonconsensual sexting among adolescents
references.abstract: Study of 1,820 adolescents validated measures of sextortion and nonconsensual sexting, finding nontrivial prevalence and modest temporal stability, and associations with cyberbullying and mental health symptoms.

references.title: Understanding online grooming: An interpretative phenomenological analysis of adolescents' offline meetings with adult perpetrators
references.abstract: Qualitative analysis of adolescents who met adult groomers offline details stages and strategies of online grooming and the psychosocial vulnerabilities exploited, informing prevention and support.

references.title: Which dynamics make online child sexual abuse and cyberstalking more emotionally impactful: Perpetrator identity and images?
references.abstract: Using 2,056 detailed episodes reported by U.S. young adults about experiences before age 18, image misuse and perpetrator identity were linked to greater negative emotional impact, underscoring harms from image-based abuse and known perpetrators.

references.title: Trends in Youth Internet Victimization: Findings From Three Youth Internet Safety Surveys 2000–2010
references.abstract: Across three U.S. national surveys of 10–17-year-olds, unwanted online sexual solicitations decreased 50% from 2000 to 2010, while online harassment increased, indicating evolving risks that require continued protection strategies.

references.title: Understanding the Decline in Unwanted Online Sexual Solicitations for U.S. Youth 2000–2010: Findings From Three Youth Internet Safety Surveys
references.abstract: Detailed analysis attributes declines in unwanted online sexual solicitations to reductions among preteens and changes in solicitation characteristics, while emphasizing that risk remains and protective efforts are needed.

references.title: Risk Factors for and Impact of Online Sexual Solicitation of Youth
references.abstract: A 1999–2000 national survey of 1,501 U.S. youth found 19% experienced unwanted online sexual solicitation; risk was higher among girls, older teens, frequent users, chat room participants, and those interacting with strangers.

references.title: Livestreaming Technology and Online Child Sexual Exploitation and Abuse: A Scoping Review
references.abstract: Synthesizing international evidence, this review documents the rise and dynamics of livestreamed child sexual abuse, methodological gaps, and challenges for prevention and law enforcement.

references.title: Victims of Technology-Assisted Child Sexual Abuse: A Scoping Review
references.abstract: Maps victim-focused evidence on technology-assisted child sexual abuse, detailing victimization patterns, impacts, and service needs to inform multi-sector responses in the digital context.

references.title: The Mental Health and Social Implications of Nonconsensual Sharing of Intimate Images on Youth: A Systematic Review
references.abstract: Systematic review of 13 studies finds nonconsensual sharing of intimate images among youth is associated with elevated depression, anxiety, suicidal ideation, and social repercussions such as bullying and victim-blaming.

references.title: Association of Sexting With Sexual Behaviors and Mental Health Among Adolescents: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
references.abstract: Across 23 studies (n=41,723), adolescent sexting was associated with sexual risk behaviors, substance use, delinquency, and internalizing problems, with stronger associations in younger adolescents.

references.title: Nonconsensual Dissemination of Sexual Images Among Adolescents: Associations With Depression and Self-Esteem
references.abstract: Using adolescent data, this study links nonconsensual dissemination of sexual images with poorer mental health indicators, including higher depressive symptoms and lower self-esteem, and examines gender and sexual orientation nuances.

references.title: Digital Sexual Violence and Suicide Risk in a National Sample of Sexual Minority Adolescents
references.abstract: Among U.S. sexual minority adolescents (n=970), threats to post or actual nonconsensual posting of sexual media were associated with markedly higher odds of suicidal ideation, planning, attempts, and self-harm.

references.title: A Global Perspective on Child Sexual Abuse: Meta-Analysis of Prevalence Around the World
references.abstract: Meta-analysis of 331 samples (n≈9.9 million) estimated substantial global prevalence of child sexual abuse, with higher self-reported rates among girls than boys, underscoring the magnitude of the problem.

references.title: Persuasion strategies and sexual solicitations and interactions in online sexual grooming of adolescents: Modeling direct and indirect pathways
references.abstract: Structural modeling in adolescents shows how online persuasion strategies and risky online behaviors contribute directly and indirectly to sexual solicitations and interactions, informing grooming prevention efforts.

references.title: The Construction and Psychometric Properties of the Questionnaire for Online Sexual Solicitation and Interaction of Minors with Adults
references.abstract: Introduces and validates a measure to assess minors’ online sexual solicitation and interactions with adults, providing a tool for surveillance and evaluation of interventions.

references.title: Outcomes of Image-Based Sexual Abuse Among Young People: A Systematic Review
references.abstract: This 2010–2024 systematic review synthesizes psychological, social, educational, and occupational consequences of image-based sexual abuse among youth, documenting severe distress and life disruptions that warrant targeted prevention and support.

references.title: School-based education programmes for the prevention of child sexual abuse
references.abstract: Cochrane review of 24 trials finds school-based CSA prevention programs improve children’s knowledge and self-protective skills and may increase disclosures, with no evidence of increased anxiety, supporting educational prevention strategies.

references.title: When Is Online Sexual Solicitation of a Minor Considered Sexual Abuse? Recommendations for Victim Prevalence Surveys
references.abstract: Provides definitional guidance for classifying online sexual solicitation of minors as sexual abuse in prevalence surveys to improve measurement consistency and policy relevance.

references.title: Parental Responses to Online Sexual Grooming Events Experienced by Their Teenage Children
references.abstract: Qualitative interviews with parents of groomed adolescents reveal emotional burdens, barriers engaging schools, and a need for targeted guidance to prevent and respond to online grooming.
references.venue: European Journal of Investigation in Health, Psychology and Education

references.title: Non-Consensual Synthetic Intimate Imagery: Prevalence, Attitudes, and Knowledge in 10 Countries
references.abstract: Surveying over 16,000 respondents in 10 countries, this preprint estimates victimization and perpetration of deepfake pornography and highlights that existing laws have not eliminated harms, calling for platform and educational responses.

references.title: "Violation of my body:" Perceptions of AI-generated non-consensual (intimate) imagery
references.abstract: U.S. survey study on attitudes toward nonconsensual synthetic intimate imagery finds strong opposition to creating and sharing deepfakes, especially sexual content, with perceptions varying by relationship context and consent attitudes.

references.title: The ‘new voyeurism’: criminalizing the creation of ‘deepfake porn’
references.abstract: Analyzes the harms of creating sexually explicit deepfakes and argues for criminalization of creation and solicitation, surveying jurisdictions that have enacted offenses and proposing law-reform options.

references.title: Rising Threats of AI-Driven Child Sexual Abuse Material
references.abstract: A Pediatrics Perspectives article defining AI-generated and AI-manipulated CSAM, outlining harms to children and challenges for identification and law enforcement, and calling for coordinated legal and technical responses.

references.title: The Legal Challenges of Realistic and AI-Driven Child Sexual Abuse Material: Regulatory and Enforcement Perspectives in Europe
references.abstract: Reviews European legal frameworks addressing realistic and AI-generated CSAM, identifying enforcement challenges and proposing regulatory responses to synthetic child sexual abuse imagery.

references.title: Moderating online child sexual abuse material (CSAM): Does self-regulation work, or is greater state regulation needed?
references.abstract: Evaluates debates over platform self-regulation versus state regulation for policing CSAM online, highlighting legal and practical challenges that inform policy responses to online child sexual exploitation.

references.title: Threat of deepfakes to the criminal justice system: a systematic review
references.abstract: Systematically reviews how deepfakes impact criminal justice, including evidentiary and policing challenges and policy implications for offenses involving synthetic sexual imagery.

references.title: Rethinking the Detection of Child Sexual Abuse Imagery on the Internet
references.abstract: Presents a longitudinal measurement study of online CSAM distribution and discusses machine-learning approaches to scale detection and response—foundational context for moderating both real and AI-generated CSAM.

references.title: Artificial Intelligence and Child Abuse and Neglect: A Systematic Review
references.abstract: Surveys AI applications relevant to child abuse and neglect, including detection and investigative tools, and discusses ethical and bias considerations pertinent to CSAM moderation.

references.title: Leveraging Self-Supervised Learning for Scene Classification in Child Sexual Abuse Imagery
references.abstract: Explores self-supervised visual models to classify contextual scenes in CSAM without direct access to sensitive data, aiming to aid triage and investigation workflows.

references.title: Detecting sexually explicit content in the context of the child sexual abuse materials (CSAM): end-to-end classifiers and region-based networks
references.abstract: Compares end-to-end classification and region-based methods for detecting explicit content relevant to CSAM moderation under strict data-access constraints.

references.title: Metadata-Based Detection of Child Sexual Abuse Material
references.abstract: Proposes machine-learning approaches that use non-image metadata (e.g., file paths) to identify CSAM, addressing legal/ethical limits on training with sensitive imagery.

references.title: Learning to Break Deep Perceptual Hashing: The Use Case NeuralHash
references.abstract: Demonstrates that deep perceptual hashing used for CSAM detection can be evaded via simple transformations, underscoring limits of current client-side scanning approaches.

references.title: Artificial intelligence and child sexual abuse: A rapid evidence assessment
references.abstract: Synthesizes empirical research (2010–2024) on AI’s role in preventing/disrupting CSA and how AI is used in offending, highlighting detection challenges for CSAM including synthetic media.
references.venue: Trends & Issues in Crime and Criminal Justice (Australian Institute of Criminology)

references.title: Underage Detection through a Multi-Task and MultiAge Approach for Screening Minors in Unconstrained Imagery
references.abstract: Introduces a multi-task model for detecting whether subjects are under legal age thresholds (e.g., under-18), relevant to filtering and enforcing policies against sexualized depictions of minors.

references.title: The Malicious Technical Ecosystem: Exposing Limitations in Technical Governance of AI-Generated Non-Consensual Intimate Images of Adults
references.abstract: Maps the ecosystem of face-swap and ‘nudify’ tools and examines gaps in current governance practices—contextualizing policy challenges that also apply to child-focused offenses.

references.title: Generative ML and CSAM: Implications and Mitigations
references.abstract: A research report outlining how generative models can produce CSAM, risks from public training datasets, and technical/policy mitigations for platforms and model developers.

references.title: Guarding the Guardians: Automated Analysis of Online Child Sexual Abuse
references.abstract: Presents automated methods to categorize and analyze CSAM-related reports to reduce analyst exposure and improve triage—supporting scalable responses to growing case volumes.

references.title: "Violation of my body:" Perceptions of AI-generated non-consensual (intimate) imagery
references.abstract: Survey research on public attitudes toward non-consensual AI-generated intimate imagery, finding strong opposition to creation and sharing—contextual evidence for criminalization efforts.

references.title: Deepfakes and Digitally Altered Imagery Abuse: A Cross-Country Exploration of an Emerging form of Image-Based Sexual Abuse
references.abstract: Multi-country criminology study documenting the prevalence, harms, and legal/policing challenges of deepfake and digitally altered sexual imagery as a form of image-based sexual abuse, with implications for young people and minors.

references.title: Prevalence of Online Sexual Offenses Against Children in the US
references.abstract: National survey estimates show substantial lifetime exposure of minors to online sexual offenses and image-based sexual abuse, underscoring the scope and seriousness of technology‑facilitated harms to children.

references.title: Forensic psychological procedures in cases of technology‑facilitated sexual abuse among adolescents: A scoping review
references.abstract: Scoping review of forensic assessment procedures for adolescent victims of technology‑facilitated sexual abuse, highlighting evidence needs and risks of harm when digital evidence is involved.

references.title: The Characteristics of Virtual Child Sexual Abuse Material Offenders and the Harms of Offending: A Qualitative Content Analysis of Print Media
references.abstract: Analyzes media‑reported cases involving virtual/AI‑like CSAM to characterize offenders and document harms, reinforcing that synthetic CSAM is treated as serious sexual offending.

references.title: A deep learning framework for finding illicit images/videos of children
references.abstract: Presents a practical CSAM triage framework combining age estimation and nudity detection to help identify child sexual exploitation content, illustrating technical feasibility and constraints.

references.title: Seeing without Looking: Analysis Pipeline for Child Sexual Abuse Datasets
references.abstract: Introduces a privacy‑preserving analysis pipeline for CSAM datasets that surfaces aggregate signals without exposing images, supporting safer research and tool development.

references.title: Anogenital Findings in Confirmed Victims of Child Sexual Abuse Materials
references.abstract: Retrospective study of children documented in CSAM shows most had normal exams and limited disclosure, emphasizing that absence of physical findings does not negate serious abuse.

references.title: Metadata‑Based Detection of Child Sexual Abuse Material
references.abstract: Proposes and evaluates metadata‑only machine learning to detect CSAM at scale without accessing illegal imagery, achieving high accuracy and robustness to adversarial obfuscation.
references.venue: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing

references.title: Deepfake detection with and without content warnings
references.abstract: Experimental evidence shows people struggle to spot high‑quality deepfakes even when warned, indicating human discernment alone cannot reliably identify synthetic sexual imagery.

references.title: Threat of deepfakes to the criminal justice system: a systematic review
references.abstract: Systematic review mapping deepfake‑related threats—including sexual abuse content—to evidence integrity and enforcement, calling for stronger detection and legal responses.

references.title: Moderating online child sexual abuse material (CSAM): Does self‑regulation work, or is greater state regulation needed?
references.abstract: Reviews platform moderation of CSAM and argues that self‑regulation is insufficient, supporting stronger statutory measures given the scale and harms of CSAM online.

references.title: Designed to abuse? Deepfakes and the non‑consensual diffusion of intimate images
references.abstract: Philosophical and normative analysis of deepfakes as tools for non‑consensual intimate imagery, articulating unique victim harms and justifying serious regulatory responses.

references.title: Contrastive Language‑Vision AI Models Pretrained on Web‑Scraped Multimodal Data Exhibit Sexual Objectification Bias
references.abstract: FAccT study shows text‑to‑image systems can generate sexualized depictions when prompted with ages (e.g., ‘[age]‑year‑old girl’), evidencing risks that models sexualize minors and require strict safeguards.

references.title: Non‑Consensual Synthetic Intimate Imagery: Prevalence, Attitudes, and Knowledge in 10 Countries
references.abstract: Large cross‑national survey estimates victimization and perpetration of deepfake pornography, finding perceived harms and gaps in legislation and platform enforcement.

references.title: SneakyPrompt: Jailbreaking Text‑to‑Image Generative Models
references.abstract: Demonstrates automated prompt‑based jailbreaks that bypass safety filters to produce NSFW imagery, highlighting the ease of generating prohibited sexual content despite safeguards.

references.title: EvilPromptFuzzer: generating inappropriate content based on text‑to‑image models
references.abstract: Presents a fuzz‑testing framework that systematically crafts prompts to elicit unsafe content from T2I systems and shows commercial moderation often fails to block generated inappropriate images.

references.title: SafeGen: Mitigating Sexually Explicit Content Generation in Text‑to‑Image Models
references.abstract: Introduces an internal representation editing approach that suppresses explicit sexual content generation across prompts, improving resistance to adversarial jailbreaks.

references.title: PromptGuard: Soft Prompt‑Guided Unsafe Content Moderation for Text‑to‑Image Models
references.abstract: Proposes a universal safety soft‑prompt to moderate NSFW generations without altering inference speed, outperforming prior defenses on multiple datasets.

references.title: SoK: Facial Deepfake Detectors
references.abstract: Systematizes state‑of‑the‑art facial deepfake detectors and benchmarks generalization under varied attack scenarios, noting limitations relevant to policing synthetic sexual content.

references.title: The Mental Health and Social Implications of Nonconsensual Sharing of Intimate Images on Youth: A Systematic Review
references.abstract: Synthesizes evidence that youth victims of image‑based sexual abuse experience depression, anxiety, suicidal ideation, bullying, and school disruption—harms likely to also arise from AI‑generated sexual imagery involving minors.

references.title: Child Sexual Abuse Material facilitated by Artificial Intelligence
references.abstract: Qualitative review of how AI can facilitate production and distribution of CSAM and the current legislative gaps, arguing for stronger, harmonized responses.

references.title: Artificial intelligence and child sexual abuse: A rapid evidence assessment
references.abstract: Peer‑reviewed rapid evidence assessment (2010–2024) of AI’s role in preventing/disrupting CSA and in enabling CSA offending, identifying research gaps and policy needs.
references.venue: Australian Institute of Criminology – Trends & Issues in Crime and Criminal Justice

claim_text: As AI technology advances, it is crucial that laws evolve to protect children from new forms of exploitation.
query:
Task: Provide up to 25 scientific references relevant to the claim.
Context: California legislative press release analysis.
Claim (senator-ochoa-bogh-advocates-californians-urging-action-governor-key-legislative-issues.txt / #4): As AI technology advances, it is crucial that laws evolve to protect children from new forms of exploitation.
Constraints:
- Prioritize peer-reviewed sources.
- Include DOI when available.
- Keep abstracts to 1-2 sentence summaries if exact abstracts not available.
- Prefer recency for fast-moving topics (e.g., deepfakes, LLMs), else include seminal works.
- provider = discovery source (e.g., 'arXiv' if link is on arxiv.org; 'publisher' for publisher site);
- venue = actual journal or conference (use 'arXiv' only if there is no published venue).
- Normalize venue names to standard short forms (e.g., NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, ICCV, ECCV, AAAI, IJCAI, ACL, EMNLP, NAACL).
Output: JSON object {"references": [{provider,id,doi,title,abstract,year,venue,authors,url,is_open_access}...]}.

references.title: Prevalence of Online Sexual Offenses Against Children in the US
references.abstract: A nationally representative survey of U.S. young adults reports lifetime prevalence of various forms of online child sexual abuse, including grooming and sextortion, underscoring the scope of technology-facilitated exploitation and the need for prevention and policy responses.

references.title: Which dynamics make online child sexual abuse and cyberstalking more emotionally impactful: Perpetrator identity and images?
references.abstract: Using retrospective reports from U.S. young adults, the study finds higher emotional harm when sexual images are misused (e.g., non‑consensual sharing or sextortion) and highlights that peers and acquaintances frequently perpetrate these harms.

references.title: Learning to Break Deep Perceptual Hashing: The Use Case NeuralHash
references.abstract: This paper demonstrates practical evasion and collision attacks against a deep perceptual hashing system proposed for CSAM detection, raising reliability and safety concerns for client‑side scanning approaches.

references.title: Bugs in our pockets: the risks of client-side scanning
references.abstract: Analyzes client‑side scanning proposals for detecting CSAM and other harms, concluding they introduce serious security, privacy, and governance risks that are difficult to mitigate at scale.

references.title: Online grooming detection: A comprehensive survey of child exploitation in chat logs
references.abstract: A systematic survey of datasets and machine learning methods for detecting online grooming in chat conversations, outlining challenges and future directions for more reliable and ethical detection.

references.title: Metadata-Based Detection of Child Sexual Abuse Material
references.abstract: Proposes a CSAM detection framework trained on file‑path metadata from real investigations, demonstrating high accuracy while avoiding legal and ethical constraints of using contraband imagery in training.
references.venue: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing

references.title: Mapping the Interdisciplinary Research on Non-consensual Pornography: Technical and Quantitative Perspectives
references.abstract: A systematic mapping study synthesizing technical and quantitative research on non‑consensual pornography (including deepfakes), identifying gaps and priorities for tool development, moderation, and regulation.

references.title: Threat of deepfakes to the criminal justice system: a systematic review
references.abstract: Reviews how deepfakes undermine evidence integrity and facilitate crimes including sexual abuse imagery, emphasizing the need for legal safeguards and improved detection to maintain justice system trust.

references.title: Designed to abuse? Deepfakes and the non-consensual diffusion of intimate images
references.abstract: Philosophical analysis argues that deepfakes enable distinctive epistemic and moral harms in image‑based abuse, motivating stronger governance and accountability for synthetic media tools.

references.title: Rising Threats of AI-Driven Child Sexual Abuse Material
references.abstract: A pediatrics perspective highlighting the rapid growth of AI‑generated and AI‑manipulated CSAM, associated risks to youth, and the need for coordinated clinical, technological, and policy responses.

references.title: The Legal Challenges of Realistic and AI-Driven Child Sexual Abuse Material: Regulatory and Enforcement Perspectives in Europe
references.abstract: Analyzes European legal frameworks facing AI‑generated CSAM, discussing enforcement challenges and proposing regulatory approaches to address realistic synthetic depictions of minors.

references.title: The ‘new voyeurism’: criminalizing the creation of ‘deepfake porn’
references.abstract: Legal analysis argues for criminalizing the creation of sexually explicit deepfakes, detailing harms—including to women and girls—and recommending urgent legislative reform alongside broader measures.

references.title: A Survey of Perceptual Hashing for Multimedia
references.abstract: Comprehensive survey of perceptual hashing techniques for images and video, covering classical and learning‑based schemes and their security/robustness—core to large‑scale detection of illegal content.
references.venue: ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing, Communications, and Applications

references.title: Robustness of Practical Perceptual Hashing Algorithms to Hash-Evasion and Hash-Inversion Attacks
references.abstract: Evaluates the security of widely used perceptual hashing algorithms (PhotoDNA, PDQ, NeuralHash), finding notable robustness under certain conditions and proposing defenses to improve security.

references.title: Non-Consensual Synthetic Intimate Imagery: Prevalence, Attitudes, and Knowledge in 10 Countries
references.abstract: A 10‑country survey (n>16,000) measures victimization and perpetration of deepfake pornography, finding nontrivial prevalence and gaps in legal deterrence—implications for platform policy and law.

references.title: Comparing Machine Learning Models with a Focus on Tone in Grooming Chat Logs
references.abstract: Compares traditional ML models and a compact LLM for detecting grooming and examines how predator conversational tone affects detection, informing practical moderation strategies.

references.title: When Is Online Sexual Solicitation of a Minor Considered Sexual Abuse? Recommendations for Victim Prevalence Surveys
references.abstract: Offers measurement guidance to better capture online sexual solicitation and abuse of minors in prevalence surveys, improving comparability for policy and prevention.

references.title: Economic Evaluation of Prevention Interventions for Child Sexual Exploitation or Child Sexual Abuse: A Systematic Review
references.abstract: Synthesizes economic evaluations of CSA/CSE prevention programs, informing policymakers on cost‑effectiveness considerations when designing protections for children.

references.title: Cybergrooming Victimization Among Young People: A Systematic Review of Prevalence Rates, Risk Factors, and Outcomes
references.abstract: Systematic review indicates at least one in ten youths experience cybergrooming, identifies associated risk factors, and underscores the need for targeted prevention and support.

references.title: Characterizing the MrDeepFakes Sexual Deepfake Marketplace
references.abstract: Empirical study of a large sexual deepfake marketplace documents supply chains, enforcement gaps, and attacker practices, informing governance and platform policy responses.

references.title: Detecting Online Grooming By Simple Contrastive Chat Embeddings
references.abstract: Introduces a contrastive‑learning approach for grooming detection in chats, showing competitive performance with simpler architectures suitable for deployment.
references.venue: Proceedings of the ACM International Workshop on Security and Privacy Analytics

references.title: The rise of AI and deepfake child abuse
references.abstract: Editorial summarizing recent evidence and watchdog reports on AI‑generated child sexual abuse imagery and calling for multi‑level responses across law, platforms, and practice.
references.venue: Journal of Family and Child Health

claim_text: AB 1831 would add depictions of minors created with artificial intelligence to California’s existing child pornography laws.
query:
Task: Provide up to 25 scientific references relevant to the claim.
Context: California legislative press release analysis.
Claim (senator-ochoa-bogh-advocates-californians-urging-action-governor-key-legislative-issues.txt / #5): AB 1831 would add depictions of minors created with artificial intelligence to California’s existing child pornography laws.
Constraints:
- Prioritize peer-reviewed sources.
- Include DOI when available.
- Keep abstracts to 1-2 sentence summaries if exact abstracts not available.
- Prefer recency for fast-moving topics (e.g., deepfakes, LLMs), else include seminal works.
- provider = discovery source (e.g., 'arXiv' if link is on arxiv.org; 'publisher' for publisher site);
- venue = actual journal or conference (use 'arXiv' only if there is no published venue).
- Normalize venue names to standard short forms (e.g., NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, ICCV, ECCV, AAAI, IJCAI, ACL, EMNLP, NAACL).
Output: JSON object {"references": [{provider,id,doi,title,abstract,year,venue,authors,url,is_open_access}...]}.

references.title: Rising Threats of AI-Driven Child Sexual Abuse Material
references.abstract: Perspective outlining how AI enables creation and manipulation of child sexual abuse material, differentiating AI-generated from AI-manipulated content and discussing risks, legal considerations, and pediatric responses.

references.title: The ‘new voyeurism’: criminalizing the creation of ‘deepfake porn’
references.abstract: Legal analysis arguing that laws should criminalize not only distribution but also the creation of sexually explicit deepfakes, including harms to women and girls and policy options across jurisdictions.

references.title: What we know and don’t know about deepfakes: An investigation into the state of the research and regulatory landscape
references.abstract: Survey of deepfake research and regulation, including global developments on criminalizing deepfake pornography and implications for policy and enforcement.

references.title: The Legal Challenges of Realistic and AI-Driven Child Sexual Abuse Material: Regulatory and Enforcement Perspectives in Europe
references.abstract: Examines how European legal systems address realistic and AI-generated CSAM, highlighting enforcement gaps, evidentiary issues, and emerging regulatory approaches.

references.title: Designed to abuse? Deepfakes and the non-consensual diffusion of intimate images
references.abstract: Philosophical and ethical analysis of deepfake sexual abuse, its harms, and the limits of consent in synthetic media contexts, with implications for regulation.

references.title: A Practitioner Survey Exploring the Value of Forensic Tools, AI, Filtering, and Safer Presentation for Investigating Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM)
references.abstract: Survey of investigators on tools and AI methods used in CSAM cases, discussing effectiveness, limitations, and needs for safer presentation and automation.

references.title: Celebrity status, sex, and variation in psychopathy predicts judgements of and proclivity to generate and distribute deepfake pornography
references.abstract: Behavioral study linking demographic and psychological traits to attitudes toward and self-reported propensity to create or share deepfake pornography.

references.title: Moderating online child sexual abuse material (CSAM): Does self-regulation work, or is greater state regulation needed?
references.abstract: Analyzes platform moderation of CSAM and argues for stronger regulatory frameworks to address enforcement deficits and protect children online.

references.title: Virtual child pornography: The eroticization of inequality
references.abstract: Early ethical analysis following Ashcroft v. Free Speech Coalition, arguing that virtual child pornography contributes to broader social harms despite contested direct victimization.

references.title: Users of Online Child Sexual Abuse Material
references.abstract: Open-access review of offender profiles, motivations, and online ecosystems facilitating CSAM, with implications for policing and policy.
references.venue: Journal of Police and Criminal Psychology

references.title: Analyzing the AI Nudification Application Ecosystem
references.abstract: Empirical study of 20 web-based AI nudification services that generate non-consensual explicit imagery, documenting features, positioning, and monetization to inform policy responses.

references.title: Non-Consensual Synthetic Intimate Imagery: Prevalence, Attitudes, and Knowledge in 10 Countries
references.abstract: Cross-national survey of over 16,000 respondents on experiences and attitudes toward deepfake pornography and other synthetic intimate imagery, including policy implications.

references.title: Detecting sexually explicit content in the context of child sexual abuse materials (CSAM): end-to-end classifiers and region-based networks
references.abstract: Presents and evaluates machine learning approaches for classifying sexually explicit content as part of automated CSAM detection pipelines under strict data-access constraints.

references.title: Leveraging Self-Supervised Learning for Scene Classification in Child Sexual Abuse Imagery
references.abstract: Explores self-supervised scene classification to assist CSAM investigations without requiring direct training on sensitive images, evaluated with law-enforcement input.

references.title: Guarding the Guardians: Automated Analysis of Online Child Sexual Abuse
references.abstract: Proposes an automated tool to analyze CSAM-related reports across subject, criminality, and harm dimensions to support investigators while reducing exposure to harmful content.

references.title: Deepfake Detection by Human Crowds, Machines, and Machine-informed Crowds
references.abstract: Large-scale study comparing human and machine deepfake detection and showing combined approaches can improve accuracy, informing governance and moderation strategies.

references.title: The ‘new voyeurism’: criminalizing the creation of ‘deepfake porn’
references.abstract: Early media studies/legal analysis of virtual child pornography and digital image manipulation in light of changing legal standards and cultural contexts.

references.title: Artificial intelligence and child sexual abuse: A rapid evidence assessment
references.abstract: Peer-reviewed government report synthesizing 2010–2024 empirical research on uses of AI to prevent/disrupt CSA and ways AI is used in CSA offending, identifying evidence gaps.
references.venue: Trends & Issues in Crime and Criminal Justice (Australian Institute of Criminology)
